channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Pollock and Brancusi Join the $100 Million Club at Auction
item.description: A “drip” painting by the Abstract Expressionist sold for $181.2 million with fees, while a bronze head by the Romanian sculptor brought in $107.6 million at Christie’s, both from the S.I. Newhouse collection.
item.dc:creator: Julia Halperin, Tim F. Schneider and Zachary Small
item.media:description: “Number 7A, 1948,” by Jackson Pollock, one of the treasures of S.I. Newhouse’s collection at Christie’s on Monday night, sold for $181.2 million with fees.

item.title: Megan Fairchild’s Long, Joyful Goodbye to Ballet
item.description: After “Coppélia,” the New York City Ballet star will leave her dancing days behind. “I have an immense amount of peace about this whole thing,” she said.
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas
item.media:credit: Sabrina Santiago for The New York Times

item.title: How the Music of ‘Schmigadoon!’ Echoes Broadway’s Past
item.description: The composer Cinco Paul discusses the clever references to classic musicals everywhere you look (and hear) in his new Broadway show.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski
item.media:description: “Schmigadoon!,” a musical satire adapted from the 2021 AppleTV+ series, is now playing at the Nederlander Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: ‘Off Campus’ Promises More Hockey, More Sex
item.description: “Heated Rivalry” was a surprise sensation, and now a new hockey romance adaptation is steaming up TV. The stars are ready to be the internet’s new boyfriends.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez and Ricardo Nagaoka

item.title: Marisa Anderson’s (Tunefully) Un-American Activities
item.description: The guitarist mined the scholar Harry Smith’s LP collection, recording her takes on songs rooted in regions the United States has been in conflict with since 1970.
item.dc:creator: Will Hermes
item.category: The Anthology of UnAmerican Folk Music (Album)
item.media:credit: Dru Donovan for The New York Times

item.title: Louvre Announces Architects for Expansion That Will Include New Mona Lisa Space
item.description: A team of international architects has been selected for the project, which is expected to increase the Paris museum’s capacity by three million visitors a year.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:credit: Antoine Boureau/Hans Lucas, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The Louvre’s current gallery displaying the Mona Lisa is often packed with visitors.

item.title: Bulgaria Hopes Eurovision Win Will Show Its Strength as a Continental Player
item.description: Recent months have brought a series of changes in the Eastern European nation, which has just adopted the euro and forced the resignation of a government through major protests.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg and Boryana Dzhambazova
item.media:description: Dara, who represented Bulgaria in this year’s Eurovision Song Contest, singing during the grand final in Vienna on Saturday night.

item.title: Review: Tyshawn Sorey Unveils a Wondrous New Piano Concerto
item.description: The Philadelphia Orchestra paired Sorey’s “For Marilyn Crispell” with Bruckner’s Third Symphony, an unusual bill that flattered both composers.
item.dc:creator: Seth Colter Walls

item.title: Bobby Darin Musical Reaches a Rare Broadway Milestone: Profit
item.description: “Just in Time,” which for a year starred Jonathan Groff, is the first new musical from last season to make money for investors.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:description: A performance at last year’s Tony Awards ceremony helped bring the show to national attention.

item.title: Breaking Down Drake’s Three-Album Surprise
item.description: Our hosts react to “Iceman,” “Habibti” and “Maid of Honour.”
item.dc:creator: Jon Caramanica, Joe Coscarelli, Kate LoPresti, Sophie Erickson, Jack Belisle, Bernardo Garcia Elguezabal and Nick Pitman
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by The New York Times; Photo: Simone Joyner/Getty Images for ABA

item.title: Inside the Met Opera: Does Peter Gelb Have ‘the Most Difficult Job on Earth’?
item.description: Peter Gelb, the general manager of the Metropolitan Opera, said he is not going anywhere as he struggles with financial crises and opera’s place in a changing arts landscape.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney

item.title: Cy Twombly, From Intimate Angles
item.description: In photos by his wife, Tatiana Franchetti, whose negatives were discovered by his granddaughter, we see more personal sides to the 20th-century master.
item.dc:creator: Arthur Lubow

item.title: Isabel Leonard and Others Offer Tastings of ‘Frida y Diego’
item.description: The performers of a Met Opera production about Frida Kahlo and Diego Rivera have been promoting the show in decidedly unoperatic places, including a cemetery.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.category: El Ultimo Sueno de Frida y Diego (Opera)
item.media:description: Isabel Leonard as Frida Kahlo in a rehearsal of the opera “El Último Sueño de Frida y Diego.” This excerpt was an intermission entertainment at a Met Live program of “Eugene Onegin” at movie theaters.

item.title: Tonys 2026 Predictions: Who Will Win? And Who Should?
item.description: Our chief theater critic looks at this year’s nominees and makes some predictions (and recommendations).
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.category: Two Strangers (Carry a Cake Across New York) (Play)
item.media:description: Susannah Flood, seated, and fellow cast members in “Liberation.”

item.title: Jon Stewart Asks What ‘Daddy’ Brought Back From China
item.description: “He was gone for a couple of days in China. We were scared,” Stewart said of President Trump on Monday’s “The Daily Show.” “But now he’s home, and I’m sure he brought us all the goodies.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: At Cannes, the Movies Are Divisive and the Arguments Heated
item.description: Without strong front-runners for the Palme d’Or, every movie is getting mixed reactions, especially the sci-fi action film “Hope.”
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan
item.media:description: Michael Fassbender and Alicia Vikander at the Cannes news conference for their polarizing film, “Hope.”

item.title: ‘The Emporium’ Review: Thornton Wilder Doesn’t Make the Sale
item.description: This newly discovered play by Wilder is part picaresque, part fable, featuring a Midwestern boy who dreams of working at a department store in the big city.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:description: Joe Tapper, foreground, in “The Emporium” at the Classic Stage Company in Manhattan.

item.title: Felicity Lott, Elegant Soprano in Opera and Song, Dies at 79
item.description: Tall and self-possessed, with an air of wit and sophistication, she was superb in works by Mozart and Strauss, and was also a wide-ranging recitalist.
item.dc:creator: Tim Page

item.title: Best Enemies-to-Lovers Romance Books, According to Emily Henry
item.description: The best-selling author recommends books by Sally Thorne, Jasmine Guillory and more where the line between nemeses and soul mates becomes deliciously flimsy.
item.dc:creator: Emily Henry
item.media:credit: Image by The New York Times; Author photo by Emily Henry

item.title: ‘In the City,’ and 7 More Things to Watch on TV This Week
item.description: A new Bravo show with “Summer House” alums premieres. And a documentary on Kylie Minogue airs.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:description: Lindsay Hubbard, center, who appeared on “Summer House,” is starring in a new reality series, “In the City.”

item.title: Review: In ‘Dad Don’t Read This’ a Playwright (Maybe?) Grows Up
item.description: Eliya Smith’s disturbing teen dramedy explores the ambivalence and confusion of life on the brink of adulthood.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw

item.title: What the Dickens? Will Ferrell Plays Ghost of Epstein on ‘S.N.L.’ Season Finale
item.description: Ferrell, the “Saturday Night Live” alum, appears with James Austin Johnson as President Trump in a sendup of “A Christmas Carol.” Paul McCartney provides the music.
item.dc:creator: Dave Itzkoff

item.title: Sex, Death and ‘Devils’ at Cannes
item.description: With the main slate making few waves, the biggest moments at the festival so far involve a new horror comedy and a restored horror provocation.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan
item.category: Teenage Sex and Death at Camp Miasma (Movie)
item.media:description: The director Jane Schoenbrun, center, shares a moment at Cannes with Gillian Anderson, left, and Hannah Einbinder, stars of “Teenage Sex and Death at Camp Miasma.”

item.title: Derek Klena Brings Broadway Flair to the Savannah Bananas
item.description: Derek Klena was a successful actor with a Tony nomination to his name. But he’s found a bigger audience with the barnstorming baseball sensation.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:credit: Elias Williams for The New York Times

item.title: The Creator of ‘Shrek!’ Never Got to See the Memes
item.description: The writer and illustrator William Steig lived several lives before his book about a surly ogre became a Hollywood hit.
item.dc:creator: Brian Raftery

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Wonderful World That Almost Was: A Life of Peter Hujar and Paul Thek’
item.description: In “The Wonderful World That Almost Was,” Andrew Durbin reconstructs the coterie that surrounded the artist-lovers Peter Hujar and Paul Thek.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Jacobs
item.category: The Wonderful World That Almost Was: A Life of Peter Hujar and Paul Thek (Book)

item.title: Robert Rauschenberg, Choreographer? His Lost ‘Pelican’ Glides Again
item.description: For a short time, Rauschenberg made dances. He used roller skates, pointe shoes and parachute wings for “Pelican” (1963), now reimagined by Trisha Brown’s company.
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas and George Etheredge

item.title: Bulgaria’s Dara Wins Eurovision 2026 as Israel Comes Second
item.description: Five countries boycotted this year’s contest over Israel’s involvement, but politics took a back seat once the show in Vienna got underway.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: Dara, center, won the competition after topping both the jury and audience votes by large margins.

item.title: Here’s how the voting works.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:description: Jury votes during the final of the 2021 Eurovision Song Contest in Rotterdam, Netherlands.

item.title: As the votes are cast, it’s a celebration of Eurovision’s 70th anniversary.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:description: Members of the Finnish band Lordi, which won Eurovision 2006, at a press conference in Vienna this week.

item.title: Romania’s song gave us this year’s lyrics controversy.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: Alexandra Capitanescu, representing Romania with “Choke Me,” has said that the song is about her inner struggle with anxiety.

item.title: From Italy, a disco anthem about the joys of marriage.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: Sal da Vinci representing Italy with "Per Sempre Si” at Tuesday night’s semifinal.

item.title: Can Felicia overcome the odds for Sweden?
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: Felicia’s song for Sweden, “My System,” is the closest thing this year’s Eurovision has to a club track.

item.title: Finland, the Eurovision Favorite, Is Here for Revenge
item.description: The pop star Pete Parkkonen and the violinist Linda Lampenius performed “Liekinheitin” on a stage covered in flames.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: Linda Lampenius and Pete Parkkonen setting Eurovision on fire in Tuesday’s semifinal.

item.title: France’s Eurovision act is just as much America’s.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: Monroe is representing France with “Regarde!,” in which she shows off her operatic vocals.

item.title: Australia’s strategy: Go big. On everything.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: Delta Goodrem, representing Australia, shot to stardom as a teenager in the early 2000s starring in the wildly popular Australian soap opera “Neighbours.”

item.title: Greece’s Akylas wants it all.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: Akylas representing Greece, during a dress rehearsal for the first semifinal with his performance of “Ferto.”

item.title: Israel’s Noam Bettan Brings French Flair to Eurovision Amid Controversy
item.description: The singer performed a pop track largely in French, as the show faced boycotts from other countries over Israel’s military actions in Gaza.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: Noam Bettan of Israel performing “Michelle” in Tuesday night’s semifinal.

item.title: A Danish theater kid thinks the omens are good for him.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: Soren Torpegaard Lund, representing Denmark this year, said that musical theater and Eurovision were “natural bedfellows.”

item.title: Who Is JJ? The 2025 Eurovision Winner Kicks Off This Year’s Show
item.description: JJ, from Austria, kicked off this year’s Eurovision grand final in Vienna, as the contest celebrates its 70th anniversary.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:description: JJ of Austria, the winner of last year’s Eurovision, performing during this year’s Eurovision Song Contest in Vienna on Thursday.

item.title: Vienna has a rich pop music scene, and it isn’t just ‘Rock Me Amadeus.’
item.dc:creator: Valeriya Safronova
item.media:description: The Austrian rock band Bilderbuch, which has taken up the mantle of Austropop in the last decade.

item.title: Why are five countries skipping Eurovision, and does it matter?
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: A protest in November outside the television studios of RTÉ, the Irish state broadcaster, calling for a boycott of the 2026 Eurovision Song Contest if Israel was allowed to participate.

item.title: A Times investigation tracked Israel’s efforts to gain soft power from Eurovision.
item.dc:creator: Mara Hvistendahl and Alex Marshall
item.media:description: Yuval Raphael represented Israel at the Eurovision Song Contest last year.

item.title: How a few hundred voters could have swayed Eurovision results.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall and Mara Hvistendahl
item.media:description: Watching a screening of the Eurovision Song Contest at a community center in Tel Aviv last year.

item.title: Who Will Win Eurovision? Prediction Markets Have Their Guess.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: Linda Lampenius, representing Finland, during the first semifinal of the Eurovision Song Contest in Vienna on Tuesday.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall

item.title: Oysters, Champagne and Billionaires Buying Art
item.description: When the European Fine Art Foundation alights in Manhattan, it’s something like a billionaire version of the classic television game show “Supermarket Sweep.”
item.dc:creator: Guy Trebay

item.title: How the ‘Is God Is’ Playwright Aleshea Harris Became a Director
item.description: Aleshea Harris won acclaim for her drama “Is God Is.” When it came time for a film adaptation, she saw cinematic possibilities far beyond her play.
item.dc:creator: Salamishah Tillet
item.media:description: Kara Young, left and Mallori Johnson as twin sisters on a mission in “Is God Is,” written and directed by Aleshea Harris.

item.title: Shakira, Gracie Abrams: 9 Songs We’re Talking About This Week
item.description: Shakira and Burna Boy’s World Cup theme, Gracie Abrams’s anxious new single, Genesis Owusu’s latest rabble-rouser and more.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:description: “Hit the Wall” is the first single from Gracie Abrams’s coming album, “Daughter From Hell.”

item.title: Sarah Sherman Took Lessons on Screaming
item.description: The “S.N.L.” comedian went to a coach who “taught all these famous screamo singing metal guys how to scream without hurting themselves.”
item.dc:creator: Kathryn Shattuck
item.media:credit: Frederic J. Brown/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images

item.title: ‘Faces of Death’ Confronts Our Viewing Habits
item.description: Alissa Wilkinson, a New York Times film critic, reviews “Faces of Death” (2026).
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson, Edward Vega and Haimy Assefa

item.title: Frida Kahlo Photos: What Made the Painter a ‘Living Work of Art’
item.description: Plaster corsets and Tehuana skirts, men’s wear and spider monkeys: These are the things that made Frida Frida.
item.dc:creator: Louis Lucero II
item.category: El Ultimo Sueno de Frida y Diego (Opera)
item.media:credit: Getty Images; Ricky Rhodes for The New York Times

item.title: The Soprano Rainelle Krause's Death is a Loss of More Than Just a Role
item.description: The coloratura soprano Rainelle Krause, who died in March, was one of the most sought after performers of Queen of the Night in ‘The Magic Flute.’
item.dc:creator: David Belcher

item.title: Smithsonian Adds Back Impeachment Language to Label on Trump Portrait
item.description: The language had been removed from wall text in the National Portrait Gallery, but it’s back as the museum unveiled changes to its exhibition on U.S. presidents.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small and Deborah Lynn Blumberg
item.media:credit: Valerie Plesch for The New York Times
item.media:description: The wall text accompanying President Trump’s image in the National Portrait Gallery has been adjusted again.

item.title: Jack Douglas, Producer for Aerosmith and Lennon, Dies at 80
item.description: A onetime Beatlemaniac, he helped shape the sound of hits like “Walk This Way” and “(Just Like) Starting Over.”
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:description: Jack Douglas, center, the producer of Aerosmith’s 1977 album “Draw the Line,” during a recording session with the engineer Jay Messina, left, and Steven Tyler, the band’s lead singer.

item.title: Claudine Longet, Entertainer Who Shot Olympian Boyfriend, Dies at 84
item.description: A singer and actress, she drew wide attention for the fatal 1976 shooting of Spider Sabich. She was convicted of negligent homicide.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel

item.title: Review: Frida Kahlo and Diego Rivera, Reunited in Opera
item.description: “El Último Sueño de Frida y Diego,” Gabriela Lena Frank and Nilo Cruz’s fantasy about two titans of Mexican art, arrives at the Metropolitan Opera.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.category: El Ultimo Sueno de Frida y Diego (Opera)
item.media:description: Carlos Álvarez, left, and Isabel Leonard as Diego Rivera and Frida Kahlo in “El Último Sueño de Frida y Diego” at the Metropolitan Opera.

item.title: Washington National Opera Builds a New Chapter After Kennedy Center
item.description: The company announced that its next season would include a slate of five full-length operas at houses around the Washington area.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts

item.title: Five Action Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks include brawling teenagers, vengeful widowers and retired swordsmen.
item.dc:creator: Robert Daniels
item.media:description: Liu Fengchao in “The Butcher’s Blade.”

item.title: ‘In the Grey’ Review: Cavill and Gyllenhaal Bring the Ruckus
item.description: Guy Ritchie’s latest is a sleek, sun-drenched actioner in which morality is fuzzy but the fashion is sharp.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny

item.title: Drake Drops 3 Albums: ‘Iceman,’ ‘Habibti’ and ‘Maid of Honour’
item.description: The chart-topping Canadian rapper released an anticipated new album, “Iceman,” on Friday and two previously unannounced LPs, “Habibti” and “Maid of Honour.”
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:credit: Emli Bendixen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Between albums, Drake headlined the three-day Wireless Festival in London last summer.

item.title: Lisa Ann Walter of ‘Abbott Elementary’ Dishes It Out
item.description: During a midcareer renaissance, the “Abbott Elementary” and “Parent Trap” star is returning to stand-up, propelled by “the insanity of being a grown-ass woman.”
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik and Maddy Rotman
item.media:description: “If I think something is funny, I’ll say it, even if maybe I shouldn’t,” Lisa Ann Walter said.

item.title: Replacing a Royal Hand-Me-Down That Finally Frayed
item.description: Three years after King Charles III was crowned, the Royal Opera House in London hung plush new stage curtains bearing the monarch’s insignia — but only after the old ones gave up the ghost.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:credit: Jeremie Souteyrat for The New York Times
